quick_facts.label: Conducting body
quick_facts.value: The National Testing Agency (NTA) conducts UGC-NET on behalf of the University Grants Commission (UGC).

quick_facts.label: What it determines
quick_facts.value: UGC-NET determines eligibility in three result categories: JRF and Assistant Professor; Assistant Professor and Ph.D. admission; or Ph.D. admission only.

quick_facts.label: Exam mode
quick_facts.value: The June 2025 cycle was conducted as a computer-based test (CBT). Candidates should confirm the mode in the bulletin for their own cycle.

quick_facts.label: Test structure
quick_facts.value: In the June 2025 bulletin, the test had 50 Paper I MCQs (100 marks) and 100 subject-specific Paper II MCQs (200 marks), completed in one 180-minute session without a break.

quick_facts.label: Core qualification route
quick_facts.value: For the June 2025 cycle, a UGC-recognized master's degree or equivalent with at least 55% for General/Unreserved/General-EWS candidates, or 50% for OBC-NCL, SC, ST, PwD and third-gender candidates, was the standard eligibility route.

quick_facts.label: JRF age rule
quick_facts.value: For the June 2025 cycle, JRF applicants had to be no more than 30 years old on June 1, 2025; Assistant Professor and Ph.D.-admission categories had no upper age limit. Age cut-offs and relaxations are cycle-specific.

sections.heading: What UGC-NET is for
paragraphs: UGC-NET is India’s national eligibility test for Indian nationals seeking one of three outcomes in universities and colleges: Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) with Assistant Professor eligibility, Assistant Professor eligibility with Ph.D. admission, or Ph.D. admission only. The qualifying category matters: the “Ph.D. only” outcome does not confer JRF or Assistant Professor eligibility, while the Assistant Professor-and-Ph.D. category does not confer JRF.

The test is an eligibility credential, not an appointment offer. A candidate who qualifies for Assistant Professor remains subject to the recruitment rules of the relevant university, college, or state government. JRF-qualified candidates may pursue research subject to securing placement under the applicable institution’s process; the June 2025 bulletin states that the JRF offer is valid for three years from issue of the JRF award letter.
bullets: For Category 2 and Category 3 UGC-NET Ph.D. admissions, the June 2025 rules provided 70% weight to NET marks and 30% to the university/HEI interview or viva voce; those NET marks were valid for Ph.D. admission for one year from the result date.
Applicants should treat these as cycle-specific rules and read the information bulletin published for the session in which they apply.

sections.heading: Eligibility: main routes and important limits
paragraphs: The standard June 2025 eligibility route was a master’s degree or equivalent from a UGC-recognized institution: at least 55% marks without rounding for General/Unreserved/General-EWS candidates, and at least 50% without rounding for OBC-NCL, SC, ST, PwD, and third-gender candidates. Candidates awaiting final master’s results could apply provisionally, but had to meet the required marks and complete the qualification within the stated period after the NET result.

The June 2025 bulletin also allowed a four-year/eight-semester bachelor’s degree route with at least 75% aggregate, with a 5% relaxation for specified categories. This route could lead to JRF and Ph.D. admission, but not Assistant Professor eligibility. Candidates are advised to choose their postgraduate subject; if it is not listed among NET subjects, they may choose a related subject.
bullets: JRF had a cycle-specific upper-age limit of 30 years as of June 1, 2025. The bulletin provided specified relaxations, including up to five years for women and OBC-NCL/SC/ST/PwD/third-gender candidates, and three years for an LL.M.; total relaxation on the listed grounds could not exceed five years.
There was no upper age limit for the Assistant Professor or Ph.D.-admission categories in the June 2025 cycle.

Eligibility is self-declared at application and remains provisional; NTA states that it does not verify eligibility documents during online registration, and the relevant institution verifies qualifications and category documents later.

sections.heading: Exam format and scoring
paragraphs: The June 2025 UGC-NET scheme used CBT and two compulsory objective, multiple-choice sections in one uninterrupted three-hour session. Paper I had 50 questions for 100 marks and assessed teaching/research aptitude, reasoning, reading comprehension, divergent thinking, and general awareness. Paper II had 100 questions for 200 marks and assessed knowledge in the subject selected by the candidate.

Each question carried two marks, with two marks for a correct response and no negative marking for an incorrect response. Unanswered questions received no marks. Except for language papers, the June 2025 question paper was offered in English and Hindi; the medium selected in the application could not later be changed, and the English version governed if there was a translation ambiguity.
bullets: The published syllabus for each NET subject is linked through the UGC-NET bulletin; Paper II preparation should be tied to the official syllabus for the selected subject.
In multi-shift subjects, NTA may use its stated normalization procedure and report percentile/NTA Score alongside marks as applicable.

sections.heading: How to apply
paragraphs: Applications are submitted online through the official UGC-NET/NTA portal when a cycle opens. In the June 2025 process, candidates registered using their own email address and mobile number, completed the form, uploaded a photograph and signature, paid the fee online, and saved the confirmation page. The application was treated as successful only after successful fee receipt.

Before applying, prepare identity and education details, the NET and postgraduate subject codes, category/PwD/EWS documents where applicable, and preferred examination cities. In June 2025, applicants selected four city preferences; NTA aimed to allot a city in the listed order but could allot another city for administrative reasons. Admit cards were downloaded from the website rather than sent by post.
bullets: Cycle-specific fee context: June 2025 charged ₹1,150 for General/Unreserved, ₹600 for General-EWS/OBC-NCL, and ₹325 for SC/ST/PwD/third-gender candidates, plus applicable payment-gateway charges and GST. Fees, dates, categories, and correction-window rules can change, so do not rely on these figures for a future session.
Only one application form was permitted in the June 2025 instructions; applications in other modes were not accepted.
Use only the current official bulletin and portal for deadlines, uploads, accepted IDs, fee payment, correction windows, city choices, and admit-card instructions.

sections.heading: Preparation approach grounded in the official scheme
paragraphs: Build a two-track plan because both papers contribute to aggregate performance. Use the official subject syllabus to map Paper II topics and study the Paper I capabilities named in the bulletin: teaching/research aptitude, reasoning, reading comprehension, divergent thinking, and general awareness. Allocate timed practice to the full 150-question, 180-minute CBT format rather than treating Paper I and Paper II as separate exam sittings.

Practice in a computer-based environment. The June 2025 bulletin directed candidates to NTA’s mock-test facility and also explains that candidates may later receive a limited opportunity to view recorded responses and challenge provisional answer keys for a prescribed fee. After each practice set, review weak syllabus units, accuracy, and pacing; since there was no negative marking in the cited cycle, formulate an attempt strategy only after confirming the current cycle’s marking rules.
bullets: Download and use the official syllabus for the exact Paper II subject selected in the application.
Take full-length timed mocks and rehearse on-screen navigation, marked-for-review use, and final submission.
Check the current official bulletin before relying on the 2025 medium, marking, or answer-key-challenge rules.

sections.heading: Results, cut-offs, and what qualifying means
paragraphs: UGC-NET qualification is competitive and subject/category-specific, not a simple fixed pass mark. Under the June 2025 result methodology, candidates first had to appear in both papers and achieve at least 40% aggregate for General/General-EWS or 35% aggregate for reserved categories. The number qualified for Assistant Professor eligibility was then set at 6% of candidates who appeared in both papers on the master’s-degree/equivalent basis, with category and subject allocation used to derive cut-offs.

NTA published June 2025 results in three outcome categories: JRF and Assistant Professor, Assistant Professor and Ph.D. admission, and Ph.D. only. The official June 2025 results release reported 5,269 candidates in the first category, 54,885 in the second, and 128,179 in the Ph.D.-only category. These counts describe that completed cycle, not a prediction of later cut-offs or selection chances.
bullets: Use the official subject/category cut-off document for the relevant completed cycle; required scores vary by subject, category, and cycle.
The final answer key is used to compile the result; NTA’s June 2025 bulletin did not provide for re-evaluation or re-checking after the result.
Download and preserve the scorecard/certificate from the official candidate portal, then separately meet the recruiting institution’s Assistant Professor or Ph.D. admission requirements.
